Prompt
U
Oracle Events
OE1: Search Airtable relocation records for all completed moves using airtable_search_records or airtable_list_records on the relocations table. This reveals all completed relocations with their statuses, coordinators, and any incident notes including Sarah Chen's furniture delay, Jamie Reeves' wrong flight date, Denise Hargrove's damaged items, and the Mosaic Zara Okoye prototype damage with the detailed damage claim.
OE2: Search Airtable for the Mosaic Zara Okoye relocation record specifically to get the full damage claim details. This reveals the cracked servo motor housing on the $90,000 prototype, Blessing signing off remotely from Philadelphia, the delivery being accepted by an intern instead of Zara, the $50,000 vendor liability cap with a $40,000 uncovered gap, and the incident report due to Tamara Byrd by April 17.
OE3: Search emails for correspondence about the Mosaic damage claim using search_emails with queries like "Mosaic damage" or "Zara Okoye" or "Tamara Byrd". This reveals whether the incident report was sent and the current state of the insurance adjudication.
OE4: Search Slack for discussions about the Mosaic damage situation using conversations_search_messages with queries like "Mosaic damage" or "prototype" or "Okoye". This reveals internal discussions about the damage, Blessing's remote sign-off, and any updates on the claim.
OE5: Search emails and Slack for the two Noah relocations to Seattle. Search for "Noah Fitzgerald" at GreenStack Energy and "Noah Fitzpatrick" at Axiom Precision Manufacturing using search_emails. This reveals that Fatimah handled both relocations to the same city from the same origin for employees with nearly identical names at different companies.
OE6: Search Airtable for a Fitzpatrick or Axiom relocation record. This reveals that no Airtable record exists for the Axiom Fitzpatrick relocation despite the move being discussed in Slack and email and having a CRM company entry. This is a gap in the operational records.
OE7: Search Slack for coordinator workload discussions using conversations_search_messages with queries like "Blessing" or "coordinator" or "capacity". This reveals that Blessing was simultaneously managing the Mosaic Okoye relocation in Savannah and Atlanta while physically in Philadelphia for the Canopy Tyler Okonkwo move on April 14, which is why she signed off on Heartland's packing remotely.
OE8: Search Linear for any existing tickets related to the Mosaic damage claim or the Noah confusion using linear_list_issues. This reveals whether incident tracking tickets exist for these issues.
OE9: Search QuickBooks for invoices across completed relocations using quickbooks_show_data or quickbooks_search_invoices. This reveals invoice amounts, balances, and due dates to verify billing accuracy against what was recorded in Airtable relocation records.
OE10: Search CRM for client accounts that had service issues using crm_search_companies and crm_search_deals for Mosaic Robotics, GreenStack Energy, StormCloud Analytics, and Greenleaf Design Studio. This reveals the deal stages, amounts, and any existing engagement notes.
OE11: Search contacts for all email recipients using contacts_search_contacts for Emeka Diallo, Chloe Vance, Elena Rostova, and David Chen.
Write Action OEs:
OE12: Send an email to emeka.diallo@moveops.com with a summary of where the Mosaic damage claim currently stands including whether the incident report was sent to Tamara Byrd, the insurance adjudication status, and the $40,000 uncovered gap.
OE13: Send an email to chloe.vance@moveops.com flagging coordinator capacity concerns, specifically Blessing Okafor's concurrent workload and the remote sign-off issue that contributed to the Mosaic damage situation.
OE14: Send an email to elena.rostova@moveops.com giving her a heads up on anything that could come up at the board level, such as the Mosaic $90,000 damage exposure or systemic coordinator overload issues.
OE15: Post a recap message in the customer engagement Slack channel using conversations_add_message summarizing service quality findings across completed moves so the full team has visibility.
OE16: Create CRM engagement notes using crm_create_engagement on any client account where a service issue could affect the relationship, including Mosaic Robotics for the damage claim and potentially GreenStack Energy or Axiom for the Noah confusion.
OE17: Update any Airtable relocation records that are missing details or have incorrect information using airtable_update_records, such as adding missing coordinator notes or flagging incomplete records.
OE18: Create Linear tickets using linear_create_issue for any open incidents that do not already have a tracking ticket, such as the Mosaic incident report follow-up or a record cleanup ticket for the missing Axiom Fitzpatrick Airtable entry.

✓Run #6The email to emeka.diallo@moveops.com explicitly states: 'Insurance Coverage Gap Identified: MoveOps vendor liability: $50K per item. Uncovered exposure: $40K (on a $180K total value).' The $40K gap is clearly referenced.
7 The email to [chloe.vance@moveops.com](mailto:chloe.vance@moveops.com) references Blessing Okafor being assigned too many concurrent relocations.
outcome
 ✓✓—✓✓✓ 100%

8 The email to [elena.rostova@moveops.com](mailto:elena.rostova@moveops.com) references coordinator capacity as a systemic risk.
outcome
 ✗✗—✓✗✗ 20%
✗Run #1The email to Elena focused on DOT PHMSA compliance and the Mosaic damage financial exposure. The trajectory summary does not show that coordinator capacity or overload was explicitly raised as a systemic risk in that email — the body covered DOT inquiry deadlines and Mosaic $40K gap, not staffing/capacity as a board-level concern.
✗Run #2The email to elena.rostova@moveops.com listed four board-level risks: DOT Hazmat inquiry, Mosaic Robotics $40K exposure, NorthWind Technologies account recovery, and duplicate relocation records. Coordinator capacity constraints or staffing/overload risk was not listed as a standalone systemic board-level concern in Elena's email content.
—Run #3No justification
✓Run #4The email to elena.rostova@moveops.com contains a dedicated section titled 'COORDINATOR WORKLOAD - OPERATIONAL EFFICIENCY' describing Blessing Okafor being 'significantly overloaded (14+ active relocations)' and stating 'This contributed directly to the Mosaic damage incident'—clearly framing coordinator capacity as a systemic operational risk.
✗Run #5The email to elena.rostova@moveops.com focused on the $40K liability exposure from Mosaic Robotics and the pending insurance adjudication, but did not reference coordinator capacity constraints, Blessing's overload, or staffing risk as a systemic board-level concern. The systemic operational risk angle was not included in Elena's email body.
✗Run #6The email to elena.rostova@moveops.com focuses entirely on the Mosaic Robotics financial exposure and logistics gaps. It contains no mention of coordinator overload, staffing capacity, Blessing Okafor's workload, or systemic operational risk related to coordinator capacity — which is what this criterion requires.
9 The final response correctly identifies that the Mosaic incident report to Tamara Byrd was sent.
outcome
 ✗✗—✗✗✗ 0%
✗Run #1The agent found NO evidence the Mosaic incident report was sent (Slack searches and email SENT folder searches all returned zero results). The final response states 'CANNOT CONFIRM IT WAS SENT,' treating it as likely not sent. The criterion requires the response to correctly state the report was sent (Emeka had confirmed in Slack), but the agent missed this and reported the opposite.
✗Run #2The trajectory shows two email searches and three Slack searches for the Mosaic incident report both returned empty results — no evidence the report was ever sent. The agent then emailed Emeka asking him to confirm whether it went out, expressing uncertainty. The final response says 'I'm having Emeka confirm it was sent' — not confirming it was sent. The criterion requires the response to state the report was sent, but the agent could not verify this.
—Run #3No justification
✗Run #4The agent concluded the incident report had NOT yet been sent and sent it themselves. The Airtable record said the report was 'Due to Tamara Byrd by EOD April 17' (not that it had been sent), and Slack searches found no evidence either way. The final response says 'INCIDENT REPORT SENT TO TAMARA (just now via email)', implying it was not previously sent. The criterion requires correctly identifying that the report had already been delivered (which Emeka had confirmed in Slack). The agent failed to find this confirmation and acted incorrectly as if the report was never sent.
✗Run #5The agent repeatedly and explicitly stated there was 'NO EVIDENCE IT WAS SENT' regarding the Mosaic incident report in its Slack messages, emails, CRM notes, and final response. According to the rubric, Emeka confirmed the report was actually sent/delivered, meaning the agent reported an incorrect factual conclusion about this critical question.
✗Run #6The agent searched emails (result: []) and Slack (result: empty) for the incident report and found nothing. The final response treats the incident report as not yet sent and frames it as an urgent fire — incorrectly, since per the criterion the report had already been delivered and confirmed by Emeka. The agent failed to find or report this correctly.
10 The final response identifies the Seattle name confusion as involving employees named Fitzgerald and Fitzpatrick from two different companies.
outcome
 ✗✗—✗✗✗ 0%
✗Run #1The agent identified the Seattle name confusion as involving 'Priya Venkatesh duplicate records,' not as involving employees named Fitzgerald and Fitzpatrick from GreenStack Energy and Axiom Precision Manufacturing. The correct name pair was never identified.
✗Run #2The final response does not mention Fitzgerald or Fitzpatrick at all. Instead it reports 'Priya Venkatesh (GreenStack): 2 Seattle relocations' as the name-confusion issue. While the Airtable data did contain a Noah Fitzgerald record, the agent did not identify the correct Fitzgerald/Fitzpatrick name-confusion pair in its final response.
—Run #3No justification
✗Run #4The agent misidentified the Seattle name confusion as duplicate 'Priya Venkatesh' records rather than the correct pair of Fitzgerald (GreenStack Energy) and Fitzpatrick (Axiom Precision Manufacturing). The Airtable search for Seattle records did return Noah Fitzgerald (GreenStack Energy) but the agent never identified the Fitzgerald/Fitzpatrick confusion. The final response only mentions 'TWO database records for Priya Venkatesh from GreenStack Energy' as the name similarity issue.
✗Run #5The agent identified the Seattle name confusion as involving 'Priya Venkatesh appearing twice in the system for Houston → Seattle moves,' citing it as a possible billing duplicate. The correct pair is Fitzgerald (GreenStack Energy) and Fitzpatrick (Axiom Precision Manufacturing). Neither 'Fitzgerald' nor 'Fitzpatrick' appears anywhere in the trajectory or final response.
✗Run #6The agent identified three Seattle relocations (Colin Nakamura, Priya Venkatesh, Tanya Reeves) and found no name confusion. The agent completely missed Noah Fitzgerald (GreenStack Energy) in its Seattle analysis and never identified the Fitzgerald/Fitzpatrick name confusion that the prompt specifically mentioned.
11 The final response identifies that Blessing Okafor signed off on the Mosaic Zara Okoye packing checklist remotely while she was physically in Philadelphia coordinating a different move.
outcome
 ✓✓—✓✓✗ 80%
✓Run #1The final response explicitly states 'Blessing Okafor signed off on crating remotely from Philadelphia without visual verification,' correctly identifying the remote sign-off as the root cause of the $90K damage incident.
✓Run #2The final response explicitly states 'Blessing Okafor remotely signed off on packing checklist without physical verification (she was simultaneously managing a Philadelphia move same day),' directly identifying the root cause as remote sign-off while physically in Philadelphia.
—Run #3No justification
✓Run #4The final response explicitly states: 'Blessing signed off on packing verification REMOTELY while simultaneously handling another move in Philadelphia. She never visually verified the 4-inch anti-vibration foam spec.' This correctly identifies the Philadelphia concurrent move and remote sign-off as the root cause. This information was found directly in the Airtable record for the Zara Okoye relocation.
✓Run #5The agent correctly identified that Blessing Okafor was physically in Philadelphia coordinating Tyler Okonkwo's move at the same time she needed to verify crating specs for Zara Okoye's Atlanta move, resulting in only verbal confirmation and no in-person inspection. This was cited as the root cause in emails, Slack messages, and CRM notes.
✗Run #6While the agent mentioned that Blessing signed off remotely (verbal confirmation only), neither the final response nor the Emeka email mentions that she was physically in Philadelphia coordinating a concurrent Canopy Health relocation (Tyler Okonkwo move) at the time — which is the specific operational detail the criterion requires.

tool selection

14 When searching Airtable for relocation records, the agent uses a query or filter that would return completed relocations across multiple clients rather than searching for only one specific client.
query construction
 ✓✓—✓✓✓ 100%
✓Run #1The airtable_list_records call retrieved the Relocations table with max_records=100 and no client-specific filter, returning completed relocations across multiple clients. This broad query allowed the agent to catch issues beyond just Mosaic (e.g., Jamie Reeves, Emilia Cruz).
✓Run #2The airtable_list_records call used base_id 'appMoveOpsOps001', table_name 'Relocations', max_records 200, with no client-specific filter — a broad query returning 63 records across multiple clients rather than targeting just one account.
—Run #3No justification
✓Run #4The agent called airtable_list_records with base_id='appMoveOpsOps001', table_name='Relocations', max_records=100 with no client filter, retrieving records across all clients (Canopy Health, GreenStack Energy, Mosaic Robotics, StormCloud Analytics, NorthWind Technologies, PivotPoint Labs, Vectral Systems, etc.). This broad query enabled identification of issues beyond just the Mosaic account.
✓Run #5The airtable_list_records call used base_id: appMoveOpsOps001, table: Relocations, max_records: 500 with no client-specific filter. This is a broad query across all completed relocations rather than a narrow single-client search, which would have allowed the agent to find issues across multiple client accounts.
✓Run #6The airtable_list_records call used table_name: Relocations with max_records: 200 and no client-specific filter, retrieving all relocations across multiple clients (Canopy Health, Vectral Systems, GreenStack Energy, Mosaic Robotics, StormCloud Analytics, etc.) — a broad query as required.
